evals.expected_output: A single @TableTest method with a scenario column, a customer tier column, an order amount column, and a discount expectation column (suffixed with ?). The table should have 3 rows, one per original test. The method body should contain a single assertEquals.

assertions.text: Output contains a @TableTest annotation
Table has at least one column name ending with '?' (e.g. 'Discount?' or 'Expected?')
Table has exactly 3 data rows (GOLD, SILVER, BRONZE)
Test method body contains only one assertEquals (or equivalent assertion) — not three
Test method body contains no if or switch statements
Table has a scenario/description column as the leftmost column

Test method has either a @DisplayName annotation or a method name that reads as a clear, descriptive title when converted from camelCase/snake_case (e.g. 'discountByCustomerTier' → 'Discount By Customer Tier'). Not a generic name like 'test1' or 'testMethod'.

@Description is present and provides context beyond what the table rows already express — such as fixed values shared by all rows, where/when the rule applies, or open questions. Does NOT merely restate the column names or summarise what the rows show.
Annotations appear in order: @DisplayName (if present), @Description (if present), @TableTest — not any other order.
@Description text uses a text block (triple-quoted string """) when the text is long, not string concatenation with +.

evals.prompt:

The exception case should use a Throws? column or be split into a separate @TableTest. The method should handle type conversion cleanly, using @TypeConverter if needed for non-standard formats. Null should be represented as a blank cell.

assertions.text: Output contains a @TableTest annotation
Null input is represented as a blank cell (not the string 'null') in the table
The empty string / exception case is handled — either via a Throws? column, assertThrows inside the method, or a separate @TableTest — rather than being silently omitted
There is a result column typed as LocalDate (or using a string representation that maps to LocalDate)
The response addresses type conversion for non-standard date formats (slash format, short year) — mentions @TypeConverter or a converter method

Test method has either a @DisplayName annotation or a method name that reads as a clear, descriptive title when converted from camelCase/snake_case (e.g. 'dateParsing' → 'Date Parsing'). Not a generic name like 'test1' or 'testMethod'.

@Description is present and provides context beyond what the table rows already express — such as supported date formats, where/when the rule applies, or open questions. Does NOT merely restate the column names or summarise what the rows show.
Annotations appear in order: @DisplayName (if present), @Description (if present), @TableTest — not any other order.
@Description text uses a text block (triple-quoted string """) when the text is long, not string concatenation with +.

evals.prompt:
We're adding TableTest to our project. The pom.xml currently has:

```xml
<dependency>
    <groupId>org.junit.jupiter</groupId>
    <artifactId>junit-jupiter</artifactId>
    <version>5.10.0</version>
    <scope>test</scope>
</dependency>
```

What do I need to add and is there anything I should watch out for?
evals.expected_output: The response should flag that JUnit 5.10.0 is below the required 5.11 minimum and recommend upgrading. It should provide the correct tabletest-junit dependency coordinates from the dependency-setup reference (not from memory). It should not guess the groupId/artifactId.

assertions.text: Response explicitly flags that JUnit 5.10.0 is below the required minimum (5.11) and recommends upgrading
Response provides the tabletest dependency with groupId 'org.tabletest' (not guessed)
Response includes 'tabletest-junit' as the artifactId
The suggested dependency includes <scope>test</scope>

evals.prompt: We're implementing a loan approval feature. The rule is: customers are approved if their credit score is above 650 and they have a stable income. But senior applicants (65+) get approved with a lower threshold of 600. We're not sure what happens if income info is missing. Can you help me work out the examples?
evals.expected_output: A markdown example table in business language with a Scenario column, Customer Age column, Credit Score column, Has Stable Income column, and Eligible? (and possibly Reason?) output columns. Should include: standard approval, standard rejection, senior threshold, below senior threshold, and at least one row marking the missing income case as open/uncertain. Column names should use domain terms, not implementation terms.

assertions.text: Output contains a markdown table (using | column | syntax)
At least one output column name ends with '?' (e.g. 'Eligible?' or 'Approved?')
Column names use domain/business language — no implementation terms like 'creditScore', 'isEligible', 'boolean', 'int'
Table includes at least one row specifically for the senior applicant (65+) threshold scenario
The missing income scenario is represented — either as a row with an open/uncertain expected value (?, TBD, or blank) or as an explicit open question note
Scenario names describe conditions ('Senior applicant at threshold') not outcomes ('Approved')

evals.prompt: I'm building an order status transition system. An order can be in states: PENDING, CONFIRMED, SHIPPED, DELIVERED, CANCELLED. The rules are getting complex — cancellation is allowed from PENDING and CONFIRMED but not after SHIPPED, and DELIVERED orders can be flagged for return within 30 days. Help me write out the examples before I start coding.
evals.expected_output: One or more example tables covering the transitions. Should use business language (not enum names as-is unless they're domain terms). Should include the 'regardless of' pattern for cases where a rule holds across multiple states. Should flag the '30 days' rule as either a threshold column or an open question. Scenario names should describe conditions, not outcomes.

assertions.text: Output contains at least one markdown table
Table covers cancellation rules — includes rows for states where cancellation is allowed and where it is not
The 30-day return window rule is addressed — either as a threshold column, a separate table, or flagged as an open question
Uses value sets {PENDING, CONFIRMED} or equivalent to express 'regardless of which starting state' for cases where a rule holds across states
At least one output column ends with '?'

evals.prompt: Midway through implementing a discount calculator I found that the 'bulk' discount and the 'loyalty' discount interact in ways we haven't agreed on. Specifically: does a customer get both discounts stacked, or only the higher one, and is there a cap? I need to work this out with the team.
evals.expected_output: An example table that makes the interaction concrete, with columns for order amount, whether bulk threshold is met, loyalty tier (or years), and the resulting discount. Should include rows that expose the ambiguity (e.g., rows where both apply) and should use an Open Questions column or blank expected values to mark what is still unresolved. Should NOT invent a resolution — the table surfaces the decision for the team.

assertions.text: Output contains a markdown table
The output does NOT silently resolve the stacking/cap ambiguity — it leaves the conflicting cases as open questions, blank cells, or explicitly marks them as unresolved
Table includes at least one row where both bulk and loyalty discounts apply simultaneously (exposing the interaction)
The unresolved decision (stacking vs. higher-only vs. cap) is explicitly surfaced — via an Open Questions column, a '?' cell, or a note
At least one output column ends with '?'

evals.prompt:
Write a TableTest for a permission check method:

boolean canPerform(Role role, Action action)

The rules are:

- GUEST can only READ

assertions.text: Output contains a @TableTest annotation
Table uses value-set syntax (curly braces like {READ, WRITE} or {READ, WRITE, DELETE}) to express rules that hold across multiple actions — rather than one row per role/action combination
Table has fewer than 9 data rows (i.e. does not enumerate every role/action combination individually)
Test method body contains no if or switch statements
Table has a scenario/description column as the leftmost column
No two rows share both the same Role value and the same boolean output — forces value set consolidation (e.g. USER can READ and USER can WRITE, both true, should be one row with {READ, WRITE}).

Test method has either a @DisplayName annotation or a method name that reads as a clear, descriptive title when converted from camelCase/snake_case (e.g. 'permissionsByRoleAndAction' → 'Permissions By Role And Action'). Not a generic name like 'test1' or 'canPerform'.

@Description is present and provides context beyond what the table rows already express — such as where permissions are checked in the request lifecycle, or open questions. Does NOT merely restate the column names or summarise what the rows show.
Annotations appear in order: @DisplayName (if present), @Description (if present), @TableTest — not any other order.
@Description text uses a text block (triple-quoted string """) when the text is long, not string concatenation with +.

evals.prompt:
Help me spec out a subscription billing feature. The rules are:
- Monthly plan costs £9.99/month; annual plan costs £95.88/year
- New subscribers get a 30-day free trial, then auto-convert to monthly
- Cancellation mid-cycle: subscriber gets a prorated refund for unused days
- Exception: if they cancel within 24 hours of a renewal charge, no refund is given
evals.expected_output: The response should produce at least two separate example tables covering distinct concerns — e.g. one for plan pricing, one for cancellation/refund rules. It should not try to combine everything into a single table. Each table should have a clear named concern, business-language column names, and at least one output column ending with '?'.

assertions.text: Output contains at least 2 separate markdown tables (not everything crammed into one table)
The tables cover distinct concerns — e.g. pricing/plans is separate from cancellation/refund rules
At least one output column in each table ends with '?'
The 24-hour no-refund exception is included as a distinct scenario row
Column names use domain/business language — no implementation terms like 'boolean', 'int', 'isProrated', camelCase identifiers

evals.prompt:
Help me spec the subscription billing feature, but we've just added two more rules:
- Annual plan subscribers who are also members of a partner loyalty programme receive a 10% discount
- Free trial is not available on annual plans

To recap the full set of rules:
- Monthly plan costs £9.99/month; annual plan costs £95.88/year
- New subscribers get a 30-day free trial, then auto-convert to monthly
- Cancellation mid-cycle: subscriber gets a prorated refund for unused days
- Exception: if they cancel within 24 hours of a renewal charge, no refund is given
- Annual plan subscribers who are also loyalty members get a 10% discount
- Free trial is not available on annual plans

I'm not sure exactly how the loyalty discount and trial period interact for the edge cases.
evals.expected_output: The response should produce multiple example tables. It should correctly recognise that loyalty (annual-only) and trial (monthly-only) don't interact at sign-up. It should surface genuinely underspecified cases — such as mid-trial upgrade to annual, joining loyalty mid-cycle, or whether cancellation refund is on discounted vs full price — as open questions rather than silently resolving them.

assertions.text: Output contains at least 2 separate markdown tables
A table or note explicitly captures that the free trial is not available on annual plans (not just implied)
At least one table row demonstrates the loyalty programme discount scenario for annual plan subscribers

The response identifies at least one genuinely underspecified interaction — such as: what happens when a trial subscriber upgrades to annual mid-trial, when a subscriber joins the loyalty programme mid-billing-cycle, or whether a cancellation refund is based on the discounted or full price. These are NOT stated in the rules and require a product decision.
The annual/loyalty/trial interaction is explicitly surfaced as an open question — via '?', 'TBD', blank expected value, or a written open question — rather than silently resolved

evals.prompt:
Help me spec a shipping cost calculator with these rules:
- Standard shipping always costs £3.99, regardless of order value or destination
- Express shipping costs £9.99 for orders under £50, and is free for orders £50 and over — but the free tier only applies in the UK
- Overnight shipping costs £14.99 and is only available for UK and Ireland destinations
evals.expected_output: An example table where Express shipping is split by the UK free-tier rule. Value sets should be used to consolidate rows with the same outcome — e.g. Express non-UK rows at/above threshold into one row, Overnight UK and Ireland into one row. Standard uses a value set for destination (all options produce the same result). Inferring 'International' as a third destination is reasonable.

assertions.text: Output contains a markdown table
Express shipping is split into at least two cases distinguishing the UK free tier (UK, order £50+, free) from the paid tier. The split is driven by the UK-only free threshold rule.

Express rows use value sets to consolidate cases with the same outcome — e.g. non-UK destinations at/above threshold in one row (such as {Ireland, Other} or {Ireland, International}), or UK at/above threshold in one row ({£50, £75}). Not enumerated as separate rows per destination or per order value when the result is identical. Any reasonable label for non-UK/Ireland destinations is acceptable (International, Other, Rest of World, etc.).
Overnight UK and Ireland are in one row using a value set ({UK, Ireland}) — not separate rows, since both have the same cost and availability.

Standard shipping destination uses a value set covering all options (e.g. {UK, Ireland, International} or {UK, Ireland, Other}) or is blank/absent — either approach is valid since destination is irrelevant to the cost. Any reasonable label for non-UK/Ireland destinations is acceptable.
At least one output column ends with '?'

evals.prompt:
Write tests for this feature:

---

Calculate weekly pay for hourly employees

We need to calculate weekly pay based on hours worked. The rules:
- Weekday hours up to 40 are paid at the base hourly rate
- Weekday hours beyond 40 are overtime, paid at 1.5× the base rate (time-and-a-half)
- Sunday hours are always paid at 2× the base rate (double time)
- Holiday hours are always paid at 2× the base rate (double time)
- Total pay cannot go below zero
- A negative hourly rate is not allowed (should be rejected)

The employee submits their hours (weekday, Sunday, holiday) and their hourly rate. The system calculates their weekly pay.
evals.expected_output: One or more @TableTest methods covering weekly pay calculation. The main pay table should have intermediate expected columns (e.g. Regular pay?, Overtime pay?, Premium pay?) for traceability, with @Description explaining the conversion formulas. Empty cells for Sunday/Holiday when not relevant. Error cases in a separate @TableTest with an expected exception column. Annotation order: @DisplayName, @Description, @TableTest.

assertions.text: The pay table includes intermediate expected columns (e.g. Regular pay?, Overtime pay?, Premium pay?) so the reader can trace which rule contributes what — not just a single total Weekly pay? column.
The error/rejection table has an expected column (e.g. 'Throws?' or 'Exception?') specifying the exception type per row — rather than hardcoding the exception type in @Description or the method name.
Overtime threshold boundary is covered: at least a value at the threshold (40) and just above (41 or similar). Not just values well below and well above.
A combined scenario is present: a row with weekday + Sunday + holiday hours, testing the composition of all hour types.
Error/edge cases covered: at least negative hours (floored at zero) and negative or missing rate (rejected).

Sunday and Holiday hour columns use empty cells (not 0) when those inputs are not relevant to a scenario — making it easier to spot which rows involve weekend/holiday hours. This is a readability preference; parameter types should be Integer (not int) to support null from blank cells.
Scenario names describe work patterns (e.g. 'Part-time', 'Five hours overtime', 'Full Sunday shift') — not 'Test case 1' or expected outcomes.
Expected values in intermediate and total columns are arithmetically correct for every row, consistent with the formulas in @Description.
Value sets are only used where all values in the set produce the same expected result (e.g. any rate × 0 = 0). Not used where results would differ.
@DisplayName is present on test methods, OR method names read as clear descriptive titles when converted from camelCase/snake_case.

@Description is present on at least the payable hours method and provides context beyond what the table rows already express — such as the conversion rules, fixed values, or application context. Does NOT merely restate the column names or summarise what the rows show.
Annotations appear in order: @DisplayName (if present), @Description (if present), @TableTest — not any other order.
@Description text uses a text block (triple-quoted string """) when the text is long, not string concatenation with +.

evals.prompt:
Write tests for this feature:

---

Travel discount on single tickets
If you purchase a single ticket for yourself as an adult or senior, you automatically receive a discount through Reis, regardless of the zones you travel in. The discount is applied when you buy single tickets in the app.
Travel is perfect for you if you travel 1–3 days a week or are not sure how much you will be traveling in the future. Perhaps you find that a period ticket is not cost-effective for you, and that you need more flexibility when you travel.
Here's how to get a discount with Reis
In short:
The discount you receive depends on how often you travel: With Reis, you will accumulate a personal discount based on the number of single tickets you have purchased in the last 30 days. The more you travel, the higher your discount will be.
You buy a single ticket as usual every time you travel.
You start to accumulate your personal discount from the first single ticket, but the first discount applies to ticket number five.
During the first 30 days, every fifth trip will take you one step higher on the discount ladder.
After the first 30 days, your discount will level off and remain the same if you have a consistent travel pattern.
If you travel less than usual, your discount may decrease.
If you travel more than usual, your discount will increase. The maximum discount is 40% off the price of a standard single ticket.
The price you pay for each ticket is therefore always adjusted to your unique travel pattern.

All children get a flat discount of 20% on single tickets.
evals.expected_output: Multiple @TableTest methods with separate tables for: discount ladder (deriving tiers from the 'every fifth trip' rule, using value sets per tier), traveller eligibility (adults/seniors vs children), and 30-day rolling window counting (with boundary testing). Should use TypeConverters for human-readable values.

assertions.text: Discount ladder, and traveller eligibility are in separate tables. Rolling window counting as a third table is desirable.
Children's flat 20% discount is represented — either as a row in the eligibility table or a separate note. It does not follow the ladder.

The discount ladder has multiple tiers derived from the 'every fifth trip' rule, with boundary values or value sets showing which ticket counts produce which discount. The maximum 40% discount is represented.
The 30-day rolling window boundary is tested: a ticket at exactly 30 days is included, at 31 days is excluded.
Eligibility uses human-readable values (yes/no or equivalent) rather than raw true/false. TypeConverter or similar mechanism mentioned.
Value sets in the tier table contain exactly the values that produce the same discount. No cross-tier contamination.
@DisplayName is present on test methods, OR method names read as clear descriptive titles when converted from camelCase/snake_case.

@Description is present on at least the class or the tier method and provides context beyond what the table rows already express — such as the discount ladder structure, eligibility rules, or application context. Does NOT merely restate the column names or summarise what the rows show.
TypeConverterSources is used for at least one human-readable conversion (yes/no, or rolling window notation).
Annotations appear in order: @DisplayName (if present), @Description (if present), @TableTest — not any other order.
@Description text uses a text block (triple-quoted string """) when the text is long, not string concatenation with +.

evals.prompt:
We need to handle order splitting — when a customer places an order for multiple items, sometimes we can't ship everything together and need to break it into separate shipments.

This happens in a few situations we keep running into. Sometimes a customer adds items to their cart where some are for store pickup and others for home delivery — obviously those can't go in the same shipment. We also see orders where some items are in stock and others are pre-orders that won't be available for a while, and we don't want to make the customer wait for everything just because one item is backordered. Then there's the inventory distribution problem — say someone orders six shirts but no single warehouse has all six. Chicago might have four, LA has one, Houston has one. We need to split across locations. And occasionally customers want items delivered to different addresses, like when they're ordering a gift for someone along with something for themselves.

When we do split, we want to minimise the number of shipments. In that shirt example, if Philadelphia and Washington each have three shirts, we should ship from those two locations rather than three. Fewer shipments means lower cost and less confusion for the customer.

One important constraint: some products are companions — like a camera body and its matching lens, which are separate line items but useless without each other. Those must always ship together from the same location.

Please help us map out these scenarios as test cases.
evals.expected_output: Multiple @TableTest methods with separate tables for: trigger detection (using set notation, including trigger interactions), shipment optimisation (using scalar quantity column), and companion constraint. The companion table should surface the 'no warehouse has both companions' case as an open question rather than silently resolving it.

assertions.text: Trigger detection, shipment optimisation, and companion constraint are in separate tables. Not one monolithic table.
At least one row where multiple triggers fire simultaneously (e.g. mixed fulfilment AND mixed stock status).
All four split triggers are covered: mixed fulfilment, mixed stock status, no single warehouse has all items, different addresses.

The 'no warehouse has both companions' case is flagged as an open question or product decision, not silently resolved as either 'error' or 'ship separately'. The requirement does NOT hint at this — the agent must recognise the ambiguity unprompted.
Companion constraint covers at least: companions at same location, companions split from non-companion items, and the ambiguous case (no location has both).
Optimisation scenarios describe the business situation (e.g. 'Consolidate: two beats three', 'Everything at one warehouse') — not 'Test case 2'.
Trigger table uses set notation ({delivery, pickup}) for closed enumerations rather than prose or lists.
Optimisation table uses a scalar count column (e.g. 'Shirts ordered') not item lists, when product identity doesn't matter for the logic.
Inventory assignments are physically valid: items are only assigned to locations that have them. Shipment counts match the assignments shown.
@DisplayName is present on test methods, OR method names read as clear descriptive titles when converted from camelCase/snake_case.

@Description is present on at least the class and provides context beyond what the table rows already express — such as the splitting rules, constraints, or application context. Does NOT merely restate the column names or summarise what the rows show.
Annotations appear in order: @DisplayName (if present), @Description (if present), @TableTest — not any other order.
@Description text uses a text block (triple-quoted string """) when the text is long, not string concatenation with +.

evals.prompt:
We're building a shopping cart and I want to make sure we have good test coverage before we ship.

The basic flow is: customers browse a product catalogue, add items to their cart with a quantity, and can remove them again. Prices come from the catalogue, not from the customer — so the cart looks up what things cost. Customers can also enter a coupon code at any point. We support three kinds of coupons: percentage off the whole cart, a fixed amount off, or a discount on a specific product. Only one coupon can be active at a time though — if they enter a new code, it replaces whatever was there before. If the code is expired or doesn't exist, we show them an error message.

At checkout we verify stock levels. If anything in the cart doesn't have enough inventory, checkout fails and we tell them what's short. You also can't check out with an empty cart, obviously.

The cart total is the sum of quantity times price for each item, minus whatever coupon discount applies, and it can never go below zero — even if someone has a massive coupon on a small order.

We want every operation to return a user-facing message so the frontend can display it — things like 'Added 2x Widget to cart', 'Coupon has expired', 'Only 3 available' and so on.

Can you help us think through the test scenarios for this?
evals.expected_output: Multiple @TableTest methods with separate tables for distinct concerns (item operations, coupon validation, cart total, checkout). Rows should be independently executable. Column names should use business language. Test data (prices, coupon rules) should be visible in the table, not hidden in setup. Should surface open questions about underspecified interactions.

assertions.text: Item operations (add/remove) are in a separate table from coupon validation. Not interleaved in one table.
Each table row is independently executable — no row depends on a prior row's result. The table expresses rules, not a sequential test script.
Item operations covered: add to empty cart, add different product, add more of same, remove product, remove unknown product, remove last item.
Coupon variations covered: valid percentage, valid fixed amount, product-specific, expired, unknown code, replace existing coupon.
A scenario where the coupon discount exceeds the cart total — total floors at zero.
Checkout covers: sufficient stock, insufficient stock, and empty cart.
Column names use business/user-perspective language (e.g. 'Message?' not 'Result?', 'Coupon code' not 'input'). The table reads as a specification a product person could review.

Test data that affects the outcome — product prices, coupon rules (type, amount, expiry) — is visible in the table via columns, @Description, or explicit setup, not silently hardcoded. The reader can trace how inputs lead to outputs.

At least one genuinely underspecified interaction is surfaced as an open question — such as: what happens to a product-specific coupon when that product is removed from the cart, or how does a percentage coupon interact with a product-specific discount. These are NOT stated in the requirements.
Cart totals are arithmetically correct: sum(quantity × price) − coupon = total, for every row in the total calculation table.
@DisplayName is present on test methods, OR method names read as clear descriptive titles when converted from camelCase/snake_case.

@Description is present on at least the class and provides context beyond what the table rows already express — such as the cart model, state management approach, or application context. Does NOT merely restate the column names or summarise what the rows show.
Annotations appear in order: @DisplayName (if present), @Description (if present), @TableTest — not any other order.
@Description text uses a text block (triple-quoted string """) when the text is long, not string concatenation with +.
